search_terms_used: mental skills running performance systematic review meta-analysis
psychological factors endurance running performance research
pacing strategy self regulation running psychology research paper
race psychology endurance athletes systematic review
mental toughness endurance athletes meta-analysis research paper DOI
self-talk endurance performance running systematic review
attentional focus running performance association dissociation endurance
coverage_notes: This collection covers the major pillars of mental performance in endurance running: perception of effort and the psychobiological model (Marcora, Pageaux), self-talk interventions (Hatzigeorgiadis meta-analysis, Blanchfield RCT, Van Raalte review), attentional focus and association/dissociation strategies (Brick 2014 review, Brick 2015 qualitative study), mental toughness (Hsieh meta-analysis), pacing psychology and decision-making under competition (Konings & Hettinga), and an overarching systematic review of psychological determinants of endurance performance (McCormick). The collection prioritizes systematic reviews and meta-analyses, supplemented by landmark empirical studies. A potential gap is pre-race anxiety and arousal regulation, which is less well-studied specifically in running contexts; the McCormick 2015 review touches on it but no standalone paper was included. Mental fatigue effects on endurance are well-represented through Marcora 2009 and Pageaux 2014.

papers.title: Psychological Determinants of Whole-Body Endurance Performance
papers.notes: Landmark systematic review identifying psychological interventions that improve endurance performance. Found consistent support for imagery, self-talk, and goal setting. Also identified mental fatigue as undermining performance and verbal encouragement/competition as beneficial. Essential reference for any coaching AI needing to understand which mental strategies have evidence behind them.

papers.title: Self-Talk and Sports Performance: A Meta-Analysis
papers.notes: Meta-analysis of 32 studies (62 effect sizes) establishing the effectiveness of self-talk in sport with a positive moderate effect size (ES = 0.48). Found self-talk more effective for fine motor tasks than gross motor tasks, and for novel vs well-learned tasks. Instructional self-talk was more effective for fine motor tasks while motivational self-talk worked for both. Foundational evidence that self-talk strategies should be part of any coaching toolkit.

papers.title: Talking yourself out of exhaustion: the effects of self-talk on endurance performance
papers.notes: First RCT to demonstrate that motivational self-talk significantly reduces perceived exertion and enhances endurance performance. Participants using self-talk increased time-to-exhaustion from 637 to 750 seconds (18% improvement) with reduced RPE at 50% isotime. Grounded in the psychobiological model, this directly shows that coaching runners to use motivational self-talk cues can meaningfully improve race outcomes.

papers.title: Self-talk: Review and sport-specific model
papers.notes: Introduces a sport-specific model of self-talk based on dual-process theory distinguishing System 1 (automatic, emotionally driven) from System 2 (deliberate, controlled) self-talk. Identifies five key components critical to self-talk interventions. Provides the theoretical framework for understanding how and when self-talk interventions work, enabling a coaching AI to recommend appropriate self-talk strategies for different racing situations.

papers.title: Attentional focus in endurance activity: new paradigms and future directions
papers.journal: International Review of Sport and Exercise Psychology
papers.notes: Comprehensive review of 35+ years of research on association vs dissociation in endurance activity. Key finding: elite athletes tend to perform better with associative (body-monitoring) focus, while recreational athletes prefer and benefit from dissociative strategies. Proposes a new working model of attentional focus that goes beyond the simple association/dissociation dichotomy. Critical for advising runners on what to focus on during races and training.

papers.title: Metacognitive processes in the self-regulation of performance in elite endurance runners
papers.notes: Qualitative interview study with 10 elite endurance runners revealing that metacognitive strategies (planning, monitoring, reviewing, evaluating) are central to cognitive control during racing. Developed an integrative metacognitive framework incorporating attentional focus dimensions. Directly applicable to coaching: elite runners actively plan and adjust their mental strategies throughout a race rather than passively experiencing thoughts.

papers.title: Mental fatigue impairs physical performance in humans
papers.notes: Landmark RCT demonstrating that 90 minutes of demanding cognitive work reduced subsequent cycling time-to-exhaustion by 15% (640s vs 754s control) despite no differences in cardiorespiratory or musculoenergetic responses. The effect was mediated entirely by higher perceived exertion. Foundational paper for the psychobiological model; directly relevant to coaching advice about pre-race mental preparation, avoiding cognitive drain before competition.

papers.title: The psychobiological model of endurance performance: an effort-based decision-making theory to explain self-paced endurance performance
papers.notes: Theoretical paper presenting the psychobiological model of endurance performance, proposing that pace regulation is an effort-based decision-making process where performance termination occurs when perceived effort exceeds motivation. Integrates perception of effort, potential motivation, knowledge of distance, and prior experience into a unified framework. Essential theoretical foundation for understanding why psychological interventions work in endurance sport.

papers.title: Effects of mental toughness on athletic performance: a systematic review and meta-analysis
papers.journal: International Journal of Sport and Exercise Psychology
papers.notes: Meta-analysis of 16 studies finding a moderate-to-high correlation between mental toughness and athletic performance (r = 0.36). Key moderator finding: the relationship was strongest in combat sports (r = 0.73), moderate in ball sports (r = 0.30), and similar in endurance sports (r = 0.32). Adults showed stronger effects than adolescents (r = 0.41 vs 0.20). Confirms mental toughness is a meaningful predictor but not the sole psychological factor for endurance success.

papers.title: Pacing Decision Making in Sport and the Effects of Interpersonal Competition: A Critical Review
papers.notes: Critical review of 75 papers (2000-2017) on how opponents and competition influence pacing decisions. Found that competitor behavior is an essential determinant of exercise intensity regulation, but athletes' responses vary by competitive context and physical state. Emphasizes that pacing is a complex decision-making process integrating both internal (fatigue, effort) and external (opponents, environment) factors. Directly relevant for race strategy advice in coached running.
